html:
Per Brahe the Younger (Abrahamsson), born on February 18, 1602, at Rydboholm Castle in Rydbo, Östra Ryd parish, Uppland, and died on September 12, 1680, at Bogesund Castle in Uppland, was a Swedish count of Visingsborg, major general, and Lord High Constable from 1640 until his death. He was the largest landowner in Sweden. He was the son of Abraham Pedersson Brahe (thus, the grandson of Per Brahe the Elder) and Elsa Gyllenstierna.

He previously served as Governor-General of Finland. He worked to promote development in Finland, including contributing to the founding of the Royal Academy of Turku and several cities. At the Royal Academy of Turku, he himself became the first chancellor in 1646. Brahe established schools, and Viborg received a gymnasium (secondary school). Per Brahe the Younger made significant contributions. He founded the town of Gränna within his county and built Brahehus. He also founded the cities of Kajana, Kristinestad, and Brahestad (named after himself). At his own expense, he improved the fortifications of Kajaneborg Castle.

== Biography ==

Between 1618 and 1625, Brahe undertook study trips abroad. In 1626, he became a chamberlain in the service of Gustav II Adolf. At the University of Giessen (1618-1621), he also took lessons in fencing, dancing, and playing the lute. The lute songbook that he began in 1620 is a memento from that time. From 1626 to 1628, he participated in campaigns in Prussia, serving close to the king. In 1628, Brahe was appointed colonel and given command of the Småland cavalry. The following year, he became marshal at the Diet and a member of the committee meeting in 1630. That same year, he became a member of the Royal Council before once again accompanying the king's campaigns to Germany. He participated in the capture of Würzburg but was subsequently sent home to take his seat on the council. During the Thirty Years' War, Würzburg was captured by Swedish troops under Gustav II Adolf in 1631. In 1632, Brahe became an assessor at the Svea Court of Appeal, and the following year he inherited the Visingsborg estate, with its 747 taxable units, from his uncle Magnus Brahe. From 1633 to 1634, Brahe also served as Axel Oxenstierna's assistant and deputy in Germany, but in 1635 he was sent to Prussia as the head of the Swedish delegation tasked with negotiating a peace treaty with Poland. The agreement reached at Stuhmsdorf in 1635, which cost Sweden control of the Prussian ports in exchange for a 26-year truce, was well-received in Sweden but strongly disapproved of by Axel Oxenstierna, further exacerbating an existing rift between the two men. This disagreement may have arisen after Gabriel Gustafsson Oxenstierna prevented Brahe from obtaining the position of Lord High Chancellor that he desired. After Axel Oxenstierna returned to Stockholm in 1636, the situation worsened and was resolved by appointing Brahe as Governor-General of Finland.

=== "The Count's Time" in Finland === In 1637, he was appointed Governor-General of Finland, and in 1641, Lord High Justice. As Governor of Finland, he contributed to the founding of cities such as Tavastehus and Nyslott (1639), Brahestad, Kristinestad, Kristina, and Villmanstrand (1640), Kajana (1651) in northern Karelia, and Veckelax Nystad (1653). He also played a role in the relocation of Helsinki to a location closer to the sea. During his tenure, a new postal service was established in Finland. In 1640, he participated in the founding of the Royal Academy in Åbo (which was later moved to Helsinki in 1828 and now operates as the University of Helsinki). On February 3, 1641, Brahe founded Finland's first elementary school in the small town of Nykarleby, but the school was relocated to Vasa in 1684. During his service in Finland, he was granted the so-called Brahelinna province in Savolax and the lordship of Kajana. The expression "I grevens tid" (literally, "in the count's time") is considered to refer to his period as governor.

=== Defense of Southern Sweden === Following Denmark's declaration of war in June 1657, which initiated Charles X Gustav's first Danish War, Per Brahe organized the defense of Småland and Halland against Christian Ulrik Gyldenløwe's Danish army in Scania. After assembling and training a Swedish army in Markaryd in southern Sweden, Brahe led the Swedes into Scania and defeated, among other things, a group of "Snapphanar" (guerilla fighters) near Båstad and attacked Ängelholm. After the battle of Ängelholm against forces led by Axel Urup, which was indecisive, Brahe and his army withdrew to Halmstad. Reinforcements arrived there, and the Swedes marched south again to aid Laholm. The Danish forces under Gyldenløwe were defeated at the Battle of Genevadsbro and withdrew from Halland. Brahe then handed over command to Gustaf Otto Stenbock, who led the main Swedish army in southern Sweden and Scania for the remainder of the war during the autumn and winter until the end of the war with the Treaty of Roskilde.

=== Visingsborg and Brahehus === Within the county of Visingsborg, the Count had Visingsborg Castle expanded. He also built Brahehus and renovated Västanå Castle. Together, these three castles formed a castle triangle, in the center of which he established the town of Brahe-Grenna in 1652. At Visingsborg, the Count maintained a small army of approximately 200 men, consisting of farmers and cavalry. During battles, pipers and drummers served as signalers for the infantry, while trumpeters and drummers signaled for the cavalry. The Count also had a chamber music ensemble at his court. In Röttle, south of Grenna, several small industries were established.

How certain are we about this information?

People from the era of Swedish Great Power are extensively documented in archives and contemporary publications. Most information about who they were and what they did is well-established; the uncertainty lies in interpretation and motivation.
tldr: Per Brahe the Younger was a powerful Swedish nobleman in the 17th century who owned vast amounts of land and held significant influence in both Sweden and Finland. He built cities, schools, and fortifications, particularly during times of war.
lattlast:
Per Brahe the Younger was a very important figure in Sweden during the 17th century. He held the title of Count, which meant he owned vast amounts of land and held high positions within the state. He served as Colonel and Lord High Constable, meaning he was a senior leader who assisted the king in governing the country.

While working in Finland, Brahe made many important contributions to its development. He helped establish several towns, built schools, and founded a university in Åbo (now Helsinki University). He also played a role in improving fortifications to protect the region.

During wartime, he led Swedish armies to defend southern Sweden against Danish forces. He was also a significant builder who oversaw the expansion of castles and the creation of new towns, such as the town of Gränna. He invested heavily in both defense and culture by employing musicians at his court.
